skill.name: defi-protocol-templates
skill.description: Provides Solidity templates and implementation guidance for staking, AMM, governance, lending, and flash loan protocol designs.
skill.summary: Create starting points for common DeFi smart contract patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static script, external command, C2, and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Solidity syntax, Markdown code fences, and DeFi terminology. No prompt injection, network exfiltration, filesystem access, or executable automation was found in SKILL.md. The remaining risk is semantic: the skill presents simplified DeFi contracts as production-ready, which could lead users to deploy unsafe financial code without review.

medium_findings.title: Production-Ready Claim for Unaudited DeFi Templates
medium_findings.description: The skill describes the Solidity examples as production-ready, but the included contracts are simplified templates for high-value financial systems. Users could deploy them without sufficient audit, testing, or threat modeling.
medium_findings.recommendation: Reframe the examples as educational starting points and require security review before mainnet deployment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The production-ready wording is explicit, and the file contains deployable Solidity examples. The risk depends on user behavior, so this is a marketplace safety concern rather than confirmed malware.

medium_findings.title: Raw ERC20 Calls in Financial Templates
medium_findings.description: The staking, AMM, and flash loan examples call ERC20 transfer, transferFrom, and approve directly. This can mishandle non-standard tokens and should use hardened token transfer helpers.
medium_findings.recommendation: Use SafeERC20 patterns and document token compatibility assumptions in every template.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The raw ERC20 calls are directly visible in the cited code. They are common in examples, but they are risky in production DeFi templates.

medium_findings.title: AMM Template Omits User Price Protection
medium_findings.description: The AMM swap and liquidity functions do not include minimum output, minimum shares, deadline, or oracle-aware protections. This exposes users to slippage and MEV risks if copied directly.
medium_findings.recommendation: Add explicit slippage, deadline, reserve validation, and MEV risk guidance before presenting the template for production use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The AMM functions are present and lack user-supplied protection parameters. This is a clear smart contract design gap, though not malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Governance Template Is Incomplete for Real Execution
medium_findings.description: The governance contract records votes but leaves proposal execution as a placeholder. It also lacks quorum, timelock, proposal payload validation, and execution target controls.
medium_findings.recommendation: Mark the governance section as illustrative and require quorum, timelock, payload, and execution controls for real protocols.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code shows vote accounting and a placeholder execution comment. The missing controls are material for production governance systems.

low_findings.title: Static Script Findings Are Solidity Require Statements
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged Solidity require statements as dynamic script loading. These lines are input checks inside smart contracts, not JavaScript require calls or dynamic module loading.
low_findings.recommendation: Do not treat Solidity require guards as executable host-side script loading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every cited line is within a Solidity code block and uses Solidity require semantics. No host language dynamic import or require call is present.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown Syntax
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged Markdown backticks and code fences as shell execution. These are documentation delimiters and inline file references, not Ruby or shell command execution.
low_findings.recommendation: Classify these lines as documentation formatting, not executable command behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown fences or an inline resource path. No shell interpreter, process execution, or command string appears.

low_findings.title: C2 and Weak Cryptography Findings Are Terminology Matches
low_findings.description: The high-risk keyword matches occur in Solidity imports, contract names, token fields, proposal descriptions, and DeFi best practices. No evidence found of command and control behavior or weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.recommendation: Dismiss these keyword-only detections unless paired with network callbacks, persistence, obfuscation, or actual weak cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are explained by DeFi and Solidity context. I found no network endpoint, encoded payload, persistence mechanism, or cryptographic algorithm implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unaudited Financial Contract Templates
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes templates for staking, AMMs, governance, and flash loans. These are high-impact financial components and should not be deployed unchanged.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Require explicit audit, testing, and deployment warnings for all DeFi contract examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited range contains multiple complete Solidity examples for financial protocols. The danger is misuse of simplified templates, not malicious skill behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct Token Transfer Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Several templates use direct ERC20 transfer, transferFrom, and approve calls. This pattern can fail with non-standard tokens and should be hardened.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Use audited token helper libraries and handle non-standard ERC20 behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The direct token calls are explicitly present. This is a known risky production pattern in DeFi examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Simplified Flash Loan Callback Flow
dangerous_patterns.description: The flash loan provider calls an arbitrary receiver callback before verifying repayment. This is expected for flash loans but needs reentrancy and receiver safety controls.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Add reentrancy controls, explicit receiver assumptions, and protocol-specific safety checks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The callback flow is visible and legitimate for flash loans. The concern is incomplete production hardening rather than a confirmed exploit.

content.value_statement: DeFi teams need repeatable starting points for common smart contract patterns. This skill provides templates and review guidance for staking, AMM, governance, lending, and flash loan designs.
actual_capabilities: Provides Solidity examples for staking rewards, AMMs, governance, and flash loans.
Identifies when DeFi protocol template guidance is relevant.
Lists common DeFi resources for staking, liquidity pools, governance, lending, and flash loans.
Recommends testing, audits, monitoring, emergency controls, and established libraries.
Explains common DeFi patterns such as TWAP, liquidity mining, vesting, multisig, and timelocks.
limitations: The included contracts are starting points, not audited production systems.
The skill does not deploy, compile, or test contracts by itself.
Protocol economics, oracle design, and chain-specific constraints require separate review.
Users must add security controls before using any template with real funds.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Staking Feature
use_cases.description: Create a first staking rewards design with reward accounting, withdrawals, and reward claims.

use_cases.title: Compare Protocol Building Blocks
use_cases.description: Review AMM, governance, lending, and flash loan patterns before choosing an architecture.

use_cases.title: Prepare Audit Requirements
use_cases.description: Turn template risks into a checklist for testing, controls, and external security review.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Template
prompt_templates.prompt: I am building a DeFi protocol. Help me choose the right template for staking, AMM, governance, lending, or flash loans.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt Staking Rewards
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt the staking rewards template for my token pair, reward schedule, and withdrawal rules. Include safety checks I must add.

prompt_templates.title: Review AMM Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review an AMM template for slippage controls, liquidity accounting, token handling, oracle needs, and MEV exposure.

prompt_templates.title: Create Audit Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an advanced security checklist for a DeFi protocol that combines staking, governance, and flash loan interactions.

output_examples.input: Help me plan a staking contract for an ERC20 rewards program.
output: A staking rewards structure with deposits, withdrawals, reward accrual, and claim flow.
A list of required safety controls, including reentrancy protection and safe token transfers.
A testing checklist for reward math, edge cases, and emergency behavior.

output_examples.input: Review an AMM design before implementation.
output: A review of reserve accounting, liquidity minting, swap pricing, and fee logic.
Warnings about missing slippage, deadline, oracle, and MEV protections.
Recommended changes before audit or deployment.

output_examples.input: Prepare governance requirements for a protocol launch.
output: A governance feature list covering proposal thresholds, voting periods, quorum, timelocks, and execution controls.
A risk checklist for vote manipulation, privileged actions, and emergency procedures.

best_practices: Treat every template as a starting point and require independent security review.
Add tests for math, access control, token behavior, reentrancy, and economic edge cases.
Use audited libraries and document every trust, oracle, and upgrade assumption.
anti_patterns: Do not deploy the example contracts unchanged with real user funds.
Do not omit slippage, deadline, oracle, or timelock controls in production protocols.
Do not rely on template code instead of protocol-specific threat modeling.

faq.question: Can I deploy these templates directly?
faq.answer: No. Treat them as educational starting points that need testing, hardening, and professional audit.

faq.question: Which protocols does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers staking, AMMs, governance tokens, lending concepts, flash loans, and common DeFi design patterns.

faq.question: Does the skill run commands or deploy contracts?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed file contains documentation and Solidity examples, not executable deployment automation.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should I review before mainnet launch?
faq.answer: Review token handling, access control, reentrancy, economic design, oracles, governance, testing, and emergency controls.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but simplified DeFi templates can cause financial loss if users treat them as production-ready.
